content.text: One of the most striking properties of learned word embeddings is that semantic relationships appear as consistent vector offsets. The "king−man+woman≈queen" example is not a one-off curiosity: the offset vector that points from "man" to "woman" is nearly identical to the offset from "king" to "queen", from "actor" to "actress", from "uncle" to "aunt". The geometry of the embedding space encodes the relationship as a direction.

attrs.alt: Left: 2D vector space showing man, woman, king, queen as a parallelogram — horizontal axis captures royalty/power, vertical axis captures gender. The analogy king−man+woman points directly to queen. Right: table of semantic and syntactic analogy categories from the Google Analogy Dataset with accuracy bars for GloVe and Word2Vec.

content.text: The standard solver is called 3CosAdd. Given the analogy "a is to b as c is to ?", it finds the vocabulary word
are excluded from the search to prevent trivial solutions.

import gensim.downloader as api
import numpy as np

wv = api.load("glove-wiki-gigaword-300")

def solve_analogy(a, b, c, wv, topn=5):
    """
    a is to b as c is to ?
    Returns the topn candidate words using 3CosAdd.
    """
    return wv.most_similar(
        positive=[b, c],
        negative=[a],
        topn=topn,
    )

# king - man + woman
print(solve_analogy("man", "king", "woman", wv, topn=3))
# [('queen', 0.85), ('princess', 0.76), ('monarch', 0.74)]

# Paris - France + Italy
print(solve_analogy("france", "paris", "italy", wv, topn=3))
# [('rome', 0.89), ('milan', 0.78), ('naples', 0.71)]

# walk - walked + ran
print(solve_analogy("walked", "walk", "ran", wv, topn=3))
# [('run', 0.88), ('sprint', 0.74), ('jog', 0.71)]
Mikolov et al. released a benchmark of 19 558 analogy pairs split into two categories:
: capitals of countries, currencies, family relationships, opposites, etc.
: adjective→adverb, plural, past tense, comparative, superlative.

# Gensim has a built-in evaluator for this dataset
result = wv.evaluate_word_analogies(
    "questions-words.txt"   # the Google analogy file
)

overall_accuracy = result[0]   # fraction of analogies solved correctly
print(f"Overall accuracy: {overall_accuracy:.1%}")

for section in result[1]:
    name  = section["section"]
    corr  = len(section["correct"])
    total = corr + len(section["incorrect"])
    print(f"  {name}: {corr}/{total} ({corr/total:.0%})")

The linear structure emerges from the training objective. When the model learns to predict "river" from "bank" and "financial" from "bank", the two senses pull the "bank" vector in different directions. Words with the same relationship — man/king, woman/queen — appear in nearly identical distributional contexts shifted by the "royalty" dimension, so the model learns the same offset for both pairs.

This is not a guaranteed outcome of any embedding method — it emerges specifically because the training corpus contains regularities in how humans write about gendered roles, countries and their capitals, and so on. The geometry of the embedding space is a mirror of the geometry of language use.
: if "Bishkek" (capital of Kyrgyzstan) appears only 100 times in the corpus, its vector is noisy and the capital–country analogy breaks.
: "bank" has a mixed vector; analogies involving polysemous words often fail.
: a model that memorised the test words during training scores high without having learned the relationship. Test words should be held out from hyper-parameter tuning.
: an alternative formula multiplies cosine similarities instead of adding vectors and often outperforms 3CosAdd on the same model.
: the analogy "man:doctor :: woman:nurse" reveals occupational gender bias in the training corpus (Bolukbasi et al., 2016).
: if English and French embedding spaces are aligned, the offset from "cat" to "chat" generalises — you can translate unseen words by applying the offset.
: analogy probing on a domain corpus tells you whether entity relationships (drug:side-effect, gene:protein) were captured during training.
: after training embeddings on a new corpus, solving a handful of known analogies is a fast check that training converged correctly.

Word embedding analogies are the property that arithmetic operations on word vectors correspond to meaningful semantic operations. In a well-trained embedding space, the vector offset between related word pairs — man and king, woman and queen — is nearly identical, so the arithmetic king − man + woman ≈ queen holds in the high-dimensional vector space. This parallelogram structure emerges from the statistical regularities in large training corpora: words that appear in similar contexts relative to each other end up with similar vector offsets.

The analogy property is not a design goal of word2vec or GloVe — it is an emergent consequence of the distributional hypothesis (words that appear in similar contexts have similar meanings) combined with training on very large corpora. The result is that the geometry of the embedding space encodes human-interpretable relationships: gender, country-capital, verb tense, comparative and superlative forms, and dozens of other relation types, all as consistent vector directions.

The analogy property is significant for three reasons. First, it provides an interpretable diagnostic for embedding quality: if a set of embeddings correctly solves capital-country analogies, it has learned a meaningful 'country' dimension, which makes it plausible that other geographic relationships are also encoded. Second, it enables transfer learning: once you identify the vector direction corresponding to 'present → past tense', you can apply it to unseen verbs. Third, it revealed structural biases in word embeddings that were invisible before — the analogy 'man:doctor :: woman:nurse' makes gender-occupation stereotypes measurable and auditable.

For the broader field of NLP and generative AI, analogy structure demonstrated that dense neural representations could encode complex semantic relationships as linear structure — a property that later proved central to how large language models represent factual knowledge. The 'analogy arithmetic' in word2vec is an early and interpretable instance of the linear structure that researchers now probe in large language models to understand what they know and how they represent it.
Benefits of Analogy Arithmetic in Embeddings
Analogy tests provide a cheap, interpretable way to verify that a specific relationship (grammar, geography, gender) is encoded before deploying the embeddings.

Once the offset for a relationship is known, it can be applied to word pairs unseen during evaluation: knowing walk → walked, the offset can translate 'run' to 'ran' without any labelled training data.
After aligning embedding spaces across languages with a linear rotation, translation becomes nearest-neighbour search — the analogy structure transfers across languages.
Analogy arithmetic makes bias quantitative: the projection of occupation word vectors onto the gender direction measures how strongly an occupation is gendered in the training corpus.
Accuracy on the Google Analogy Dataset is a standard benchmark that enables comparison across embedding models, training corpora, and dimensionalities.
3CosAdd accuracy is bounded by vocabulary size.
The method searches over all vocabulary words; for large vocabularies (100K+), even a correct vector offset may be defeated by a closer impostor word.
Words with multiple senses (bank, light, spring) have mixed vectors that make analogies involving them unreliable.
Words with fewer than ~100 training occurrences have high-variance vectors; analogies involving them fail at higher rates.
The parallelogram relationship holds approximately, not exactly. The true nearest neighbour to v_king - v_man + v_woman is queen most of the time, but not always.
Models whose training data overlaps with the analogy test set (the Google Analogy Dataset) score artificially high. Always evaluate on held-out sets.
Debiasing methods that project out a gender direction can create new biases or reduce performance on downstream tasks. No single debiasing approach is universally accepted.
How It Works: From Co-occurrence to Linear Structure
The link between training objective and linear structure is best understood through the GloVe model (Pennington et al., 2014). GloVe explicitly optimises the difference of log co-occurrence ratios:
where X_{ik} is the number of times word i co-occurs with context word k. The key insight is that the log ratio of co-occurrence probabilities encodes relationships:

These ratios vary along exactly the dimensions that distinguish the two concepts. When GloVe embeds this ratio information into inner products, the resulting vectors encode the contrast as a direction in embedding space. The same mechanism applies to gender: words that co-occur disproportionately with male-gendered words end up with a specific directional component that the analogy arithmetic exploits.

Word2Vec's skip-gram model achieves the same effect via a different route: it trains a classifier to distinguish true context words from random negative samples. Mikolov et al. (2013) showed that the resulting weight matrices encode point-wise mutual information (PMI) implicitly — specifically, the learned product w_i · c_j approximates PMI(w,c) − log(k) where k is the negative-sampling rate. PMI differences between word pairs encode the same log-ratio structure as GloVe.
How to Implement: Full Analogy Pipeline

# pip install gensim numpy
import gensim.downloader as api
import numpy as np
from typing import Optional

# ── Load pre-trained embeddings ───────────────────────────────────────────────
# GloVe (300d, trained on Wikipedia + Gigaword, 6B tokens)
wv = api.load("glove-wiki-gigaword-300")

# ── 3CosAdd: standard analogy solver ─────────────────────────────────────────
def solve_3cosadd(a: str, b: str, c: str, wv, topn=5, oov="<unk>"):
    """
    Solve: a is to b as c is to ?
    3CosAdd formula: argmax cosine(v_d, v_b - v_a + v_c)
    Excludes a, b, c from results to prevent trivial solutions.
    """
    try:
        return wv.most_similar(positive=[b, c], negative=[a], topn=topn)
    except KeyError as e:
        return [(oov, 0.0)]

# ── 3CosMul: often outperforms 3CosAdd ────────────────────────────────────────
def solve_3cosmul(a: str, b: str, c: str, wv, topn=5, epsilon=1e-9):
    """
    3CosMul: argmax [cos(d,b) * cos(d,c)] / [cos(d,a) + epsilon]
    More robust when cosine similarities are small.
    """
    try:
        return wv.most_similar_cosmul(positive=[b, c], negative=[a], topn=topn)
    except (KeyError, AttributeError):
        return solve_3cosadd(a, b, c, wv, topn)

# ── Evaluate on a custom analogy set ─────────────────────────────────────────
def evaluate_analogies(pairs, wv, solver=solve_3cosadd):
    """
    pairs: list of (a, b, c, expected_d) tuples
    Returns accuracy (exact match on top-1 answer).
    """
    correct = 0
    for a, b, c, expected in pairs:
        results = solver(a, b, c, wv, topn=1)
        if results and results[0][0].lower() == expected.lower():
            correct += 1
    return correct / len(pairs) if pairs else 0.0

SEMANTIC_TEST = [
    ("man",    "king",   "woman",  "queen"),
    ("france", "paris",  "italy",  "rome"),
    ("dollar", "usa",    "yen",    "japan"),
    ("good",   "better", "bad",    "worse"),
    ("uncle",  "man",    "aunt",   "woman"),
]

acc_add = evaluate_analogies(SEMANTIC_TEST, wv, solve_3cosadd)
acc_mul = evaluate_analogies(SEMANTIC_TEST, wv, solve_3cosmul)
print(f"3CosAdd accuracy: {acc_add:.1%}")
print(f"3CosMul accuracy: {acc_mul:.1%}")
Example 1: Building a complete analogy probe library

import numpy as np
import gensim.downloader as api
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

wv = api.load("glove-wiki-gigaword-100")   # 100d, faster for demos

@dataclass
class AnalogyResult:
    a: str; b: str; c: str
    predicted: str; score: float; correct: Optional[str]
    is_correct: bool

def solve(a, b, c, expected=None, wv=wv, topn=1):
    try:
        candidates = wv.most_similar(positive=[b, c], negative=[a], topn=topn)
        predicted, score = candidates[0]
    except KeyError:
        predicted, score = "OOV", 0.0
    return AnalogyResult(
        a=a, b=b, c=c,
        predicted=predicted, score=score, correct=expected,
        is_correct=(predicted.lower() == (expected or "").lower())
    )

# Semantic analogies
print("=== Semantic ===")
for r in [
    solve("paris",   "france",  "rome",       "italy"),
    solve("tokyo",   "japan",   "beijing",    "china"),
    solve("king",    "man",     "queen",      "woman"),
    solve("actor",   "man",     "actress",    "woman"),
]:
    status = "✓" if r.is_correct else "✗"
    print(f"  {r.a}:{r.b}::{r.c}:? → {r.predicted} ({r.score:.2f}) {status}")

# Syntactic analogies
print("\n=== Syntactic ===")
for r in [
    solve("walk",    "walked",  "run",        "ran"),
    solve("quick",   "quickly", "slow",       "slowly"),
    solve("cat",     "cats",    "dog",        "dogs"),
    solve("good",    "better",  "bad",        "worse"),
    solve("big",     "biggest", "small",      "smallest"),
]:
    status = "✓" if r.is_correct else "✗"
    print(f"  {r.a}:{r.b}::{r.c}:? → {r.predicted} ({r.score:.2f}) {status}")
Example 2: Measuring and visualising the gender direction

import numpy as np
import gensim.downloader as api
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA

wv = api.load("glove-wiki-gigaword-100")

# ── 1. Identify gender direction ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# Use multiple definitional pairs for robustness
gender_pairs = [
    ("man", "woman"), ("he", "she"), ("boy", "girl"),
    ("father", "mother"), ("king", "queen"), ("uncle", "aunt"),
]
gender_vecs = [wv[m] - wv[f] for m, f in gender_pairs]
gender_dir = np.mean(gender_vecs, axis=0)
gender_dir /= np.linalg.norm(gender_dir)

# ── 2. Project occupation words onto the gender direction ─────────────────────
occupations = [
    "doctor", "nurse", "engineer", "receptionist", "lawyer",
    "secretary", "surgeon", "midwife", "programmer", "teacher",
    "pilot", "librarian", "ceo", "homemaker", "astronaut",
]
available = [w for w in occupations if w in wv]
scores = {w: float(wv[w] @ gender_dir) for w in available}

sorted_scores = sorted(scores.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
print("Most 'female' (negative projection):")
for w, s in sorted_scores[:5]:
    print(f"  {w}: {s:.3f}")
print("Most 'male' (positive projection):")
for w, s in sorted_scores[-5:]:
    print(f"  {w}: {s:.3f}")
# Most 'female': homemaker, nurse, receptionist, midwife, secretary
# Most 'male':   ceo, astronaut, pilot, programmer, engineer

# ── 3. Hard debiasing: project out gender component ───────────────────────────
def debias_vector(v, bias_dir):
    """Remove the component along the bias direction."""
    return v - (v @ bias_dir) * bias_dir

for w in ["doctor", "nurse", "engineer"]:
    original_score  = wv[w] @ gender_dir
    debiased_vec    = debias_vector(wv[w], gender_dir)
    debiased_score  = debiased_vec @ gender_dir
    print(f"{w:15}: original={original_score:.3f}  debiased={debiased_score:.6f}")

Cross-lingual search uses analogy-based alignment between embedding spaces: find a linear rotation W that maps English vectors to French vectors, then perform semantic search across languages by comparing rotated English queries to French document embeddings.

import numpy as np
from sklearn.preprocessing import normalize

# ── Seed dictionary (word pairs known to be translations) ─────────────────────
SEED = [
    ("cat", "chat"), ("dog", "chien"), ("house", "maison"),
    ("run", "courir"), ("fast", "rapide"), ("good", "bon"),
    ("king", "roi"), ("woman", "femme"), ("city", "ville"),
]

def align_embeddings(src_wv, tgt_wv, seed_pairs):
    """
    Learn orthogonal rotation W such that src_vectors @ W ≈ tgt_vectors.
    Uses SVD (Procrustes analysis).
    """
    src_words, tgt_words = zip(*seed_pairs)
    available = [(s, t) for s, t in seed_pairs if s in src_wv and t in tgt_wv]
    if not available:
        raise ValueError("No seed pairs found in both vocabularies")
    s_words, t_words = zip(*available)

    S = np.stack([src_wv[w] for w in s_words])  # (n, d)
    T = np.stack([tgt_wv[w] for w in t_words])  # (n, d)
    S = normalize(S); T = normalize(T)

    U, _, Vt = np.linalg.svd(T.T @ S)
    W = U @ Vt   # orthogonal matrix: (d, d)
    return W

# ── Cross-lingual nearest neighbour translation ───────────────────────────────
def translate(word, src_wv, tgt_wv, W, topn=3):
    """
    Translate 'word' from src to tgt language using embedding alignment.
    """
    if word not in src_wv:
        return []
    rotated = normalize(src_wv[word].reshape(1, -1)) @ W  # (1, d)
    return tgt_wv.similar_by_vector(rotated[0], topn=topn)

# This requires two separate gensim word vector objects for each language.
# Example usage (if you have fasttext vectors for English and French):
# en_wv = KeyedVectors.load_word2vec_format("cc.en.300.vec")
# fr_wv = KeyedVectors.load_word2vec_format("cc.fr.300.vec")
# W = align_embeddings(en_wv, fr_wv, SEED)
# print(translate("science", en_wv, fr_wv, W))
# → [('science', 0.95), ('scientifique', 0.81), ('recherche', 0.73)]
print("Cross-lingual alignment: align embeddings with SVD Procrustes rotation")
print("Translation = nearest neighbour search in aligned target space")
Not excluding query words from the candidate set

# 3CosAdd can fail when cosine similarities are concentrated near 0 or 1.
# Test both and pick by held-out validation:
import gensim.downloader as api
wv = api.load("glove-wiki-gigaword-100")

pairs = [("walk", "walked", "run", "ran"), ("cat", "cats", "dog", "dogs")]
for a, b, c, exp in pairs:
    r_add = wv.most_similar(positive=[b, c], negative=[a], topn=1)
    r_mul = wv.most_similar_cosmul(positive=[b, c], negative=[a], topn=1)
    print(f"{a}:{b}::{c}:?")
    print(f"  3CosAdd → {r_add[0][0]} ({r_add[0][1]:.3f})")
    print(f"  3CosMul → {r_mul[0][0]} ({r_mul[0][1]:.3f})")
Treating high benchmark accuracy as evidence of unbiased embeddings

# High analogy accuracy ≠ unbiased embeddings.
# The analogy dataset includes gender pairs like "man:king :: woman:queen"
# that REINFORCE gender stereotypes when used to verify embeddings.
# Correct audit approach: test for UNWANTED analogies

wv_check = None  # replace with loaded wv
try:
    import gensim.downloader as api
    wv_check = api.load("glove-wiki-gigaword-100")
    # These analogies reveal bias if the top answer is a gender-stereotyped word
    bias_probes = [
        ("man",  "doctor",      "woman",   None),   # expect: doctor, not nurse
        ("he",   "programmer",  "she",     None),   # expect: programmer, not secretary
    ]
    for a, b, c, _ in bias_probes:
        results = wv_check.most_similar(positive=[b, c], negative=[a], topn=3)
        print(f"  {a}:{b}::{c}:? → {[r[0] for r in results]}")
except Exception:
    print("(wv not loaded in this environment)")
Use multiple definitional pairs to identify bias directions.
A single (man, woman) pair is noisy; average over 6-10 definitional pairs to get a robust gender direction.
3CosMul often outperforms 3CosAdd on the Google Analogy Dataset; compare on a validation split before choosing.
Report per-category accuracy, not just overall.
Semantic and syntactic analogies have very different difficulty profiles; reporting aggregate accuracy hides important differences.
Use sentence-transformers for downstream tasks, not raw analogies.
Analogy accuracy is a diagnostic metric, not an optimisation target. Models optimised for analogies are not necessarily better at classification or retrieval.
Run the WEAT (Word Embedding Association Test) and occupation gender bias audits as part of every embedding release process.
Use higher-dimensional embeddings for analogy tasks.
300d embeddings solve analogies significantly more accurately than 50d or 100d embeddings, because more dimensions allow more relationship directions to be orthogonal.

The computational cost of analogy evaluation is dominated by the nearest-neighbour search over the full vocabulary. For a vocabulary of V words and embedding dimension d, computing all cosine similarities to the target vector costs O(V · d). For V=400 000 and d=300, that is 120 million multiply-add operations per analogy — about 50ms in NumPy. Over a full 19 558-pair evaluation, this is roughly 15 minutes without batching.

attrs.latex: \text{Analogy solve cost} = O(V \cdot d) \text{ per query}

content.text: In practice, gensim batches these computations with BLAS routines, bringing the total evaluation time down to 2-5 minutes. For production systems that need analogy-like semantic search in real time, approximate nearest-neighbour libraries (FAISS, ScaNN, Annoy) reduce the per-query cost to O(log V) or O(V^0.5) with small accuracy trade-offs.

attrs.alt: Left: occupational gender bias revealed by analogy arithmetic in GloVe. Right: cross-lingual transfer via linear rotation of embedding spaces

content.text:
# Running the full Google Analogy evaluation with gensim
import gensim.downloader as api

wv = api.load("glove-wiki-gigaword-300")

# Download the analogy file from: https://code.google.com/archive/p/word2vec/
# File: questions-words.txt  (included in word2vec package)
# Then:
# accuracy, sections = wv.evaluate_word_analogies("questions-words.txt")
# print(f"Overall accuracy: {accuracy:.1%}")
# for s in sections:
#     n = len(s["correct"]) + len(s["incorrect"])
#     a = len(s["correct"]) / n if n > 0 else 0
#     print(f"  {s['section']:40s}: {a:.1%}")

# Alternative: evaluate on your own analogy file
# File format (one analogy per line):
# : capital-world                  ← section header
# Athens Greece Baghdad Iraq
# Athens Greece Bangkok Thailand
# ...
print("See gensim.KeyedVectors.evaluate_word_analogies for full usage")

Word embeddings trained on uncurated text corpora encode harmful biases that propagate into downstream applications. The analogy structure makes these biases explicit and measurable — a double-edged property. It enables bias auditing (a clear benefit) but also confirms that the embeddings are systematically biased (a deployment risk). Applications that use raw word embeddings for hiring recommendations, credit scoring, or medical triage must undergo bias audits before deployment, using tools like WEAT or the word analogy bias tests shown above.

From a reliability perspective, embedding drift is a production risk: if the embedding model is updated (e.g., retrained on a newer corpus), the analogy structure may shift in ways that break downstream applications that depended on specific vector offsets. Pin the embedding version in production and run analogy regression tests after any update.
Run the Word Embedding Association Test for gender, race, and disability bias before any production deployment.
Pin the embedding model version; analogy structure can shift significantly between GloVe releases or corpus updates.
For production, use fastText (subword) embeddings to handle OOV words gracefully; raw word2vec/GloVe return zero vectors for OOV.
Automated bias tests identify patterns, but human review is required to determine whether a specific bias is acceptable for a given use case.
Word embedding analogies are an emergent property of distributional training: similar co-occurrence ratios produce similar vector offsets, which manifest as parallelogram geometry.
The standard solver is 3CosAdd: find the word d that maximises cosine(v_d, v_b - v_a + v_c), excluding a, b, and c from the candidate set.
3CosMul (multiply cosine similarities rather than add vectors) often outperforms 3CosAdd; test both on your specific relation type.
The Google Analogy Dataset (19 558 pairs) is the standard benchmark; report semantic and syntactic accuracy separately.
Analogy accuracy is bounded by vocabulary size, embedding dimensionality, and the frequency of the query words in the training corpus.
Analogy arithmetic reveals gender, racial, and occupational biases in embedding spaces; always run WEAT audits before production deployment.
Cross-lingual transfer exploits analogy structure: a linear rotation (SVD Procrustes) aligns two embedding spaces so translation becomes nearest-neighbour search.
For production systems, use approximate nearest-neighbour search (FAISS, HNSW) to bring query latency from O(V·d) to O(log V·d).
